title: Batik on wool with soy wax
subtitle: Applying melted soy wax resist to wool fabric before dyeing with acid dyes to create patterned colour
excerpt: Batik on wool uses a wax resist to prevent dye from reaching selected areas of the cloth. Soy wax is used here because it melts and removes at a lower temperature than traditional beeswax or paraffin, reducing the risk of felting the wool during the wax removal stage. This entry covers applying the wax, dyeing, and removing the resist to reveal the pattern.
sourceNotes: Batik on wool with soy wax synthesised from general batik technique references adapted for protein fibres. Soy wax is a lower-temperature alternative to traditional batik waxes suitable for heat-sensitive fibres.

glossaryTerms.definition: Melted wax applied to fabric before dyeing to prevent dye from penetrating those areas. When the wax is removed after dyeing, the protected areas show the original cloth colour.
A class of synthetic dye used on protein fibres (wool, silk, alpaca) in an acidic dye bath. Acid dyes produce bright, washfast colours and are the standard choice for domestic wool dyeing.

The fine lines of dye that penetrate through small cracks in the wax resist, producing a characteristic veined pattern in batik work. In wool batik, some crackle is inevitable and is generally considered part of the aesthetic.
Any material applied to fabric to prevent dye penetration. In wax batik, the resist is the solidified wax layer on the surface of the cloth.

attrs.title: Hot wax safety
attrs.body: Melted wax burns on contact. Never leave the wax pot unattended; keep a lid nearby to smother any flame. Do not heat above 90°C.

content.text: Batik on wool is more delicate than batik on cotton because wool is heat-sensitive and susceptible to felting. Soy wax melts and solidifies at a lower temperature than paraffin or beeswax, which allows the
to be removed by melting at temperatures the wool can tolerate without felting. The
bath must be kept at 75°C or below during dyeing to avoid felting the waxed cloth. Some
is unavoidable on wool: the fabric has more surface texture than cotton, so some dye always finds its way through small cracks in the

items.name: Woven or knitted wool fabric, pre-washed and damp
Acid dye powder, one or two colours
Small metal wax pot or double boiler
Old towels and iron for wax removal

content.text: Melt the soy wax in a small pot to 70-75°C. It should be liquid and clear, not smoking. Lay the damp wool fabric flat on a smooth surface. Apply the melted wax quickly with a wide brush or a tjanting tool, covering all areas you want to protect from the dye. Work swiftly: soy wax solidifies faster than paraffin. The wax must penetrate through both fabric surfaces to provide an effective resist: hold the fabric up to the light after application and check that the wax area is translucent, not opaque white on one side.
Step 2: Allow the wax to cool

Leave the waxed fabric flat at room temperature until the wax is completely solid, approximately 15-20 minutes. Do not chill in the refrigerator: rapid chilling causes the wax to crack more than room-temperature cooling. Once solid, the waxed fabric can be handled carefully without breaking the resist.

Prepare an acid dye bath according to the dye manufacturer's instructions. Add white vinegar or citric acid to acidify the bath. Heat the dye bath to 70-75°C maximum: above this temperature the wool begins to felt under the wax, and the wax may partially melt and bleed. Submerge the waxed fabric gently and hold at temperature for 30-45 minutes, stirring very carefully to avoid cracking the resist. Rinse in water of the same temperature.

Sandwich the dyed fabric between several layers of old towels or newspaper. Press with a hot iron: the heat melts the soy wax out of the fabric and into the absorbent layers. Change the towelling as it saturates with wax. Repeat until no more wax transfers. Wash the fabric by hand in warm water with a small amount of washing-up liquid to remove the last wax residue. The pattern will be revealed as the undyed areas appear in the original fabric colour.

items.problem: The wax resist areas have taken up dye and show colour rather than remaining undyed
items.cause: The wax did not penetrate fully through the fabric, leaving the back surface exposed to dye
items.fix: Apply wax at a slightly higher temperature (80°C) so it flows more freely and penetrates the fabric weave. Check for full penetration against light before dyeing.

items.problem: The fabric has felted during dyeing
items.cause: The dye bath temperature exceeded 75°C, or the fabric was agitated too vigorously
items.fix: Keep the dye bath below 75°C and handle the waxed fabric as little as possible during dyeing. Use a single slow stir every 10 minutes rather than continuous movement.

items.problem: The wax is difficult to remove after dyeing
items.cause: Soy wax has a narrow melting range and is more difficult to remove from dense fabrics than paraffin
items.fix: Iron at a higher heat setting and use more layers of absorbent material beneath the fabric. Boiling water immersion for 30 seconds may help for stubborn residue, but only if the fabric has been dyed at a low enough temperature that felting is not a concern.
